annotations.text: Several factors are likely to have contributed: difficulties with adherence, an interaction between rifampin and ritonavir, and problems with dosing.
When it is determined that antiretroviral therapy has failed in a child, a full assessment of the possible reasons for failure should be conducted.

Although we know of no randomized, controlled trials to guide decisions about the optimal viral load at which to switch to second-line therapy, there are some principles of switching antiretroviral therapy after treatment failure that may provide guidance.
In this patient, multiple protease-inhibitor mutations were detected. Since the child had not previously received an NNRTI, a simple switch to two NRTIs and an NNRTI might be appropriate.
Whether the failed regimen was an NNRTI-based regimen or a protease-inhibitor–based regimen will determine which active agents should make up the new regimen.
This case illustrates the problems that can occur when HIV infection in pediatric patients is not diagnosed and treated properly.
Viral resistance in children may be prevented by early identification of HIV-infected children and the initiation of treatment.

Regular monitoring of treatment responses should be in place. Laboratory investigations, such as viral-load monitoring and resistance testing, must become more affordable to programs to guide treatment decisions.

Families may initially be resistant to telling the child. They may feel guilty about having transmitted the infection to the child, and they are often worried that the child might disclose the diagnosis to others.
Peer support groups are often helpful, particularly with older children and adolescents.
Alternatively, a regimen that includes an NNRTI plus a newer protease inhibitor might also be effective.
HIV that is resistant to older protease inhibitors, such as lopinavir boosted with ritonavir, may still be susceptible to newer protease inhibitors, such as darunavir and tipranavir.

Darunavir is a nonpeptide protease inhibitor that binds rapidly to protease at a unique site and dissociates slowly, resulting in a binding strength that is two times as high as that of other protease inhibitors.
